metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the rubric prompt file path from `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/reviewers/plan_review_necessity.md` to `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_necessity.md` (the actual prompt library directory used by all peer criteria).
evidence: Plan Scope: `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/`: ... `reviewers/plan_review_necessity.md`
Actual directory listing shows `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/` has no `reviewers/` subdirectory
All peer rubrics (plan_review_A1.md, plan_review_asserted_capability.md, plan_review_decomp_shape.md) live in `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/`, not `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/reviewers/`
registry.py docstring line 6: `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_<id>.md`
findings.finding: The plan names the rubric prompt file as `reviewers/plan_review_necessity.md` under `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/` (i.e., `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/reviewers/plan_review_necessity.md`), but that directory does not exist. All existing plan-review rubric prompts live at `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_<id>.md` — confirmed by the `reviewers/` listing (plan_review_A1.md, plan_review_asserted_capability.md, plan_review_decomp_shape.md, etc.) and by the registry.py docstring: `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_<id>.md`.
scenarios: Implementer creates the file at the wrong path; registry `_descriptor_from_prompt` fails to load it because `get_prompt('plan-review-necessity')` looks in `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/`, not `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/reviewers/`; CI parity gate (`validate_packaged_routing`) fails with a missing prompt.
findings.suggested_fix: Place the new rubric at `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_necessity.md` — matching the existing pattern for every other plan-review criterion rubric (plan_review_A1.md, plan_review_asserted_capability.md, etc.).

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean, sufficient
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete jq proving command for the bug_tier_sanity.json artifact, e.g.: `jq -e '.runner != "exempt" and .verdict != "PASS" and (.criteria_run | map(select(.name=="necessity")) | length) > 0 and .blocking == false' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/bug_tier_sanity.json`
evidence: AC3 states the bug-tier result must show 'verdict runner != "exempt", the necessity probe ran, no blocking' but supplies no jq assertion against the committed JSON
The necessity_sanity.json half of AC3 has metric thresholds (recall 1.0, false-accept 0.0) but the bug_tier_sanity.json half has only prose requirements
findings.finding: AC3's bug-tier sanity portion commits a JSON artifact (bug_tier_sanity.json) but provides no concrete jq/grep proving command to verify the committed file actually contains the required properties (runner != 'exempt', necessity probe ran, no blocking verdict); this is a type-6 verify-command defect (prose assertion instead of structured artifact check).
scenarios: An executor commits a bug_tier_sanity.json with runner='exempt' due to a wiring bug; the AC passes because there is no structured assertion to catch the discrepancy.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC3: 'Verify with: `jq -e \'(.runner != "exempt") and ((.criteria_run // []) | map(select(.name=="necessity")) | length > 0) and (.blocking == false or .det_blocking == [])\' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/bug_tier_sanity.json` exits 0.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Convert AC5 to a concrete observable check, e.g.: `grep -qE 'bug.?review.?tier|necessity.?probe|promotion.?gate' docs/plan-review-gate.md && echo PASS` — or enumerate the required section headings that must appear.
evidence: 'documents the bug review tier and the necessity probe, and the advisory->blocking promotion gate for both' is a deliverable description, not a testable outcome
No grep or section-check command is given to verify these topics are actually present in the doc
findings.finding: AC5 ('docs/plan-review-gate.md documents the bug review tier and the necessity probe, and the advisory->blocking promotion gate') is effort-framed — it describes work to do rather than a specific observable outcome, and carries no grep/existence proving command to confirm the required content sections are present.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC5: '`docs/plan-review-gate.md` documents the bug review tier and the necessity probe, and the advisory->blocking promotion gate for both...'
scenarios: An executor writes a docs update that mentions the terms but omits the promotion gate protocol; the AC passes on human review because the check is purely subjective.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the prose AC with: '`grep -cE "bug_tier|necessity|promotion_gate" docs/plan-review-gate.md` returns ≥ 3 (one match per required topic), confirming all three topics are documented.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether a P8-too-big bug short-circuits to `run_llm=False` (preserving the existing guard) or intentionally proceeds to the 1-TURN `necessity` probe regardless of plan size — state the chosen behavior explicitly in the `plan_review_precheck` bullet.
evidence: Plan: 'run the DET floor, DOWNGRADE all DET findings to advisory (det_blocking:[] — a bug is never blocked)'
Current code (workflow_ops.py:181-199): `p8_too_big` guard short-circuits to `run_llm=False` before returning, for all non-exempt ticket types
P8 is documented as 'BLOCKS when content exceeds largest window even one-at-a-time' (det_floor.py:35-36)
ABSENCE: plan does not address whether P8-too-big bugs take a different branch or proceed to the 1-TURN necessity probe regardless of size
findings.finding: The plan says all DET findings for bugs are downgraded to advisory (`det_blocking:[]`), including P8 (too-big), but does not specify whether a P8-too-big bug short-circuits before `run_llm=True` or proceeds to the 1-TURN necessity probe. The existing non-bug code explicitly short-circuits on P8 before any LLM call; if that guard is removed for bugs, an oversized bug plan would attempt a full workflow run with `necessity` only — which may be fine for a 1-TURN criterion but is unaddressed, leaving an ordering/branching ambiguity in the implementation.
findings.location: What — piece (b): lightweight bug review tier / workflow_ops.plan_review_precheck bullet
scenarios: A bug ticket with an enormous plan description (e.g. a lengthy repro log embedded in the plan) triggers P8; under the new tier it proceeds to `run_llm=True`; the 1-TURN necessity call may succeed (single criterion, small rubric) but this is never stated as intentional
An implementer reading the plan is left to decide independently whether to preserve the P8 short-circuit for bugs or not — a high-risk ambiguity in a security-adjacent flow control decision
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'A P8-too-big bug still short-circuits (run_llm=False, no LLM call) — the DET downgrade (det_blocking:[]) applies only to the blocking verdict classification, not to the P8 size-based short-circuit branch.' OR, if the intent is to always run necessity: 'P8-too-big is also downgraded; a P8 bug proceeds to run_llm=True since necessity is a 1-TURN probe that fits in any context window.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the schema/shape of the `coverage.bug_tier` stamp (e.g. `True` boolean, or `{"ran": true, "criteria": ["necessity"]}`), where it appears in the `plan_review_precheck_output` schema, and how `plan_review_coach` receives and merges it into the final verdict's `coverage` dict (add a `with: bug_tier_coverage: ${{ steps.precheck.outputs.bug_tier_coverage }}` line to the YAML or equivalent).
evidence: Plan says: 'stamp `coverage.bug_tier`' in the precheck bullet, but gives no schema for the stamp value or merge path.

`plan_review_coach` (workflow_ops.py:528-532) builds coverage as `{'det': ctx.inputs.get('det_coverage') or {}, 'routing': ctx.inputs.get('routing') or {}, 'llm_ran': True}` — `bug_tier` is not a current input to this step.
`plan_review_precheck_output` schema is not shown, so whether `bug_tier` flows as a new top-level output key (requiring schema change) or is nested inside `det_coverage` is unspecified.
The AC assertion command checks `coverage.bug_tier` in a `bug_tier_sanity.json` but gives no schema for what value it should hold.
findings.finding: The `coverage.bug_tier` stamp has no specified data-flow path from `plan_review_precheck` through to the final `plan_review_verdict`. The precheck currently emits `det_coverage`; the verdict's `coverage` dict is assembled in `plan_review_coach` via `{'det': ctx.inputs.get('det_coverage') or {}, 'routing': ..., 'llm_ran': True}`. The plan says precheck 'stamps `coverage.bug_tier`' but never specifies (a) what shape/value the stamp takes, (b) whether it's a key inside `det_coverage` or a sibling key, and (c) how `plan_review_coach` receives and merges it into the final verdict's coverage.
findings.location: What — piece (b): lightweight bug review tier / `workflow_ops.plan_review_precheck` bullet
scenarios: An implementor must decide: is `coverage.bug_tier` a boolean flag, a string, a sub-dict? Where does it appear in the verdict JSON? Without this, the unit test AC ('precheck emits `coverage.bug_tier`') cannot be written deterministically.

The workflow YAML passes `det_coverage` from precheck to coach explicitly; a new `bug_tier` key in the precheck output needs a corresponding `with:` wire in the YAML or a schema change — both unspecified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the P8-too-big interaction for the bug tier: either (a) exempt bugs from P8 (document that bug plans are reviewed regardless of size), or (b) allow P8 to short-circuit bugs to a non-blocking advisory verdict (preserving the 'never block' invariant while avoiding the context-overflow failure mode).
evidence: Plan: 'for `ticket_type == "bug"`, run the DET floor, DOWNGRADE all DET findings to advisory (`det_blocking:[]` — a bug is never blocked), set `run_llm:True`, and stamp `coverage.bug_tier`.'
Existing precheck (workflow_ops.py:181-199): P8-too-big causes `run_llm:False` and a terminal BLOCK verdict for non-bug tickets.

The plan says `det_blocking:[]` (a bug is never blocked), but P8's too-big finding is treated as a BLOCK-tier DET finding — making `det_blocking:[]` implies P8 would also be downgraded to advisory for bugs, but then `run_llm:True` means the LLM runs on a plan it can't fit in context.

The 'never block a bug' invariant is described as 'structural (advisory posture + empty det_blocking)' but P8's blocking is a window-size guard, not a plan-quality judgment — running the LLM on a P8-flagged plan would fail.
findings.finding: The plan's bug-tier `precheck` redesign (run DET floor for bugs, set `run_llm:True`) does not specify the interaction with the P8-too-big short-circuit. Currently, bugs short-circuit BEFORE the DET floor runs (workflow_ops.py:150 — the bug exemption check precedes `det_floor.run_det_floor()`). Under the new plan, bugs run the DET floor. If P8 fires on a bug plan, the plan is silent: does the bug still proceed to the light LLM tier (forcing `run_llm:True` even when P8 says the plan is too large to review), or does P8 still short-circuit for bugs?
findings.location: What — piece (b): lightweight bug review tier / `workflow_ops.plan_review_precheck` bullet
scenarios: A bug ticket with a 50,000-token plan: P8 fires (plan too large to review). Under 'never block + run_llm:True', the LLM tier attempts to run on a plan that exceeds its context window, producing a broken review.

Implementor must decide: exempt bugs from P8 entirely (skip P8 for bugs), or short-circuit on P8 for bugs (contradicting 'run_llm:True'), or truncate the plan for bugs (not specified). Each has different behavior.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete detection/branch mechanism in `plan_review_assemble_criteria` for the bug tier: e.g. 'when `pctx.ticket_type == "bug"`, set `included = {cid for cid in effective if cid in set(registry.BUG_TIER_CRITERIA)}`' (analogous to the existing `probe` path at lines 253-257), co-located with the BUG_TIER_CRITERIA definition in the Scope section.
evidence: Plan: 'for `ticket_type == "bug"`, restrict the included LLM criteria to `registry.BUG_TIER_CRITERIA`'.

Current `plan_review_assemble_criteria` (workflow_ops.py:215-270): calls `orchestrator.assemble_context()` to get `pctx`, then `orchestrator.route_criteria(pctx)`, then builds the `included` set. No branch on `ticket_type` exists.

Since `necessity` has no `suppress_types:["bug"]`, `route_criteria` already includes `necessity` for bugs. The restriction to ONLY `necessity` (excluding all other non-suppressed criteria) requires an explicit override that is not described.
The `probe_criteria` mechanism (lines 253-257) offers an existing pattern for forcing an allowlist, but the plan does not say to reuse it for the bug tier.
findings.finding: The `plan_review_assemble_criteria` redesign for bugs — 'restrict the included LLM criteria to `registry.BUG_TIER_CRITERIA` (= `("necessity",)`)' — does not specify the detection mechanism. The current `assemble_criteria` calls `orchestrator.route_criteria(pctx)` to get the included set; for bugs the plan says to override this with `BUG_TIER_CRITERIA`. But `assemble_criteria` does not currently receive `ticket_type` as an explicit input — it calls `orchestrator.assemble_context()` internally. The plan gives no code-level protocol for how `assemble_criteria` branches on bug vs. non-bug (a conditional on `pctx.ticket_type`, a new input parameter, or a different code path), leaving the decision rule unspecified.
scenarios: Without a specified detection mechanism, an implementor might implement the bug-tier restriction as a `pctx.ticket_type == 'bug'` branch inside `assemble_criteria` OR as a new `probe_criteria`-style input — two structurally different implementations with different test implications.
The AC unit test 'assemble restricts a bug's included set to `necessity`' cannot be written without knowing which code path produces the restriction.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the `plan_review_assemble_criteria` bullet: 'Mirror the existing `probe` allowlist path (lines 253-257): when `pctx.ticket_type == "bug"`, set `included = {cid for cid in effective if cid in set(registry.BUG_TIER_CRITERIA)}`; this reuses the same allowlist override code path without a new engine branch.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that verifies necessity appears in `workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml` — e.g. assert the criterion name is present in the yaml `criteria:` list, or run the full plan-review gate (not just `rebar criteria eval`) on a fixture plan and confirm necessity is invoked (verdict references it).
evidence: Piece (a) states: 'Wire it into the production Pass-1 finder batch (workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml criteria: list, gated by when: include_necessity) so it actually runs on leaf plan reviews.'
Scope section lists: 'src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml (finder batch slot)'
No AC contains a verification step asserting the necessity criterion appears in plan-review.yaml or that a gate run (as opposed to a direct rebar criteria eval run) exercises it.
findings.finding: The plan explicitly describes 'Wire it into the production Pass-1 finder batch (workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml criteria: list, gated by when: include_necessity)' as a deliverable in piece (a), but no acceptance criterion directly verifies that this wiring was actually performed. AC 3 runs 'rebar criteria eval necessity' (a direct eval path), not a gate execution; AC 2 covers unit tests for necessity routing/registration. The yaml change could be omitted and all ACs would still pass.
findings.location: What — piece (a), last bullet; Scope section (plan-review.yaml line); Acceptance Criteria (all)
scenarios: Implementer updates all registry/routing/rubric files and passes all ACs, but forgets to add necessity to plan-review.yaml; the criterion never fires in production gate runs.
A reviewer checking ACs cannot distinguish a correctly wired gate from one where necessity was left out of the yaml.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: '`python -c "import yaml; d=yaml.safe_load(open('src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml')); assert 'necessity' in d['criteria']"` exits 0' OR extend AC 3's bug_tier_sanity to verify the gate path (not just `rebar criteria eval`) ran necessity.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm BUG_TIER_CRITERIA warrants a named exported constant given it has 1 production call-site; if no second call-site is anticipated in this story, consider inlining `frozenset({"necessity"})` at the assemble_criteria bug branch and validating it directly in the AC assertion.
evidence: Plan: 'registry.py: add BUG_TIER_CRITERIA = ("necessity",)'
Plan: 'plan_review_assemble_criteria: for ticket_type == "bug", restrict the included LLM criteria to registry.BUG_TIER_CRITERIA'
AC: 'assert r.BUG_TIER_CRITERIA==("necessity",)' — 2 reference sites total (assemble + AC assertion)
Existing constants CODEBASE_GROUNDED has 3+ production call-sites; AGENT_TIER has 2; BUG_TIER_CRITERIA has 1 production call-site
findings.finding: BUG_TIER_CRITERIA = ('necessity',) is a new exported constant with exactly 1 production call-site (the assemble_criteria bug branch), which is below the Rule-of-Three threshold for a named abstraction. The plan could inline the literal set at its single call-site and still satisfy the AC assertion (which checks the constant's value, not its existence as a contract surface). Contrast with CODEBASE_GROUNDED (3+ sites) and CANONICAL_LLM (multi-site validation + routing), which meet the Rule-of-Three.
findings.location: What — piece (b): lightweight bug review tier / registry.py: add BUG_TIER_CRITERIA = ('necessity',)
scenarios: A reviewer applying Rule-of-Three strictly would flag a 1-element tuple constant as premature abstraction when inlining frozenset({'necessity'}) at the one call-site is equally clear and equally validated by the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly what `include_necessity` is (e.g., an env-var, a `criteria_routing.json` `enabled` field, or an existing feature-flag mechanism) and specify its default value so the executor can wire it without guessing.
evidence: 'gated by `when: include_necessity`' — the plan names the condition but does not define its type (env var, config key, registry flag) or where/how it is set
No other section of the plan explains how `include_necessity` is toggled or provisioned
findings.finding: The `when: include_necessity` gate condition in `plan-review.yaml` is unspecified: the plan does not state whether `include_necessity` is an environment variable, a feature-flag config key, an existing YAML conditional pattern, or something new to be created — leaving the executor to invent the wiring mechanism.
findings.location: What — piece (a), bullet: 'Wire it into the production Pass-1 finder batch (`workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml` `criteria:` list, gated by `when: include_necessity`)'
scenarios: An executor adds the `when: include_necessity` YAML stanza but has no guidance on what controls the flag, so the criterion silently never runs in the finder batch because the flag defaults to falsy or is undefined.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: '`include_necessity` is a boolean entry in `workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml`'s top-level `feature_flags:` map (default: `true`); set it to `false` to suppress the probe in environments where LLM budget is constrained.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/index.json` regeneration (via `regenerate_prompt_index`) to the scope — it is an unenumerated consumer that must be updated whenever a new rubric file is added to `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/`.
evidence: Plan Scope names `docs/plan-review-criteria-guide.md (regenerated)` but omits `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/index.json`
`src/rebar/llm/reviewers/index.json` exists in the reviewers directory listing
registry.py references the prompt library as `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_<id>.md` with index.json as the derived selection-catalog artifact
prompts.py lines 260-274 show `index.json` is the catalog that `load_catalog`/`select_reviewers` operate on and is regenerated via `regenerate_prompt_index`
findings.finding: Adding a new prompt file to `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/` requires regenerating `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/index.json` (the derived prompt index) via the `regenerate_prompt_index` machinery. The plan enumerates regenerating `docs/plan-review-criteria-guide.md` but does not mention regenerating `reviewers/index.json`. This is an unenumerated consumer outside the `plan_review/` artifact directory.
scenarios: The new `plan_review_necessity.md` is created but `index.json` is not regenerated; the prompt machinery cannot discover the new rubric; `check_registry_coverage` and the CI parity gate fail.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
